summary: Jeff Atwood argues against the IT industry's obsession with audience metrics like pagerank, traffic numbers, and presentation ratings. He shares his own approach of deliberately ignoring these numbers, citing Conan O'Brien's 'Field of Dreams' philosophy of focusing on creating content you believe in rather than optimizing for audience data. The post suggests that overthinking metrics leads to wasted energy and added stress. Instead, creators should trust their instincts and focus on making something they themselves would want to consume.
key_points: The IT industry has an unhealthy tendency to fetishize audience metrics like pagerank, backlinks, and attendance numbers
Conan O'Brien's success came from ignoring research and viewer data, focusing solely on making content he found funny
Obsessing over metrics adds unnecessary stress and distracts from the actual creative work
The best strategy is to create content you'd want to consume yourself rather than optimizing for external validation
Metrics have value but are easily overanalyzed at the expense of trusting your own judgment
money_quotes: We have a tendency to fetishize audience metrics in the IT industry.
I couldn't even tell you how many readers I have, or what my presentation ratings were.
Audience metrics aren't the reason I write, and they aren't the reason I present. They're incidental.
I don't look at research. I don't look at who's watching, or when they're watching. I've never been interested in any of that. I'm interested in doing what I think is funny.
If I'm looking at spreadsheets and time-lapse studies of viewing patterns, I think I'm wasting my time.
The day I care about keeping my blog readers happy is the day I'll stop blogging. Who needs the added stress?
Instead of obsessing over who does and doesn't link to you, concentrate on writing a blog entry you'd like to read.
You should trust your gut more than any metrics.
key_insight: Trust your creative instincts over audience metrics—focus on making something you'd want to consume, and the audience will follow.
